title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: Three scenarios call distributed API: Data lives KK different machines — privacy governance constraints prevent pooling, site can publish small summary. Data one machine doesn’t fit memory — process chunk chunk accumulate. Streaming data — new batches arrive time refitting scratch expensive. three solved primitive: chunk produces vcmm_ss summary; summaries additive; fit computed aggregate.

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: Theorem 1 Jalili Lin (2025): partition data KK chunks, compute sufficient-statistics summary chunk, sum summaries, fit. result bit-equivalent (BLAS summation noise) fitting pooled data. summaries lose information — sufficient statistics VCMM likelihood. summary chunk kk stores (ak,bk,Ck,(Z⊤Z)k,(Z⊤y)k,(X⊤Z)k,nk) \bigl(a_k,\; b_k,\; C_k,\; (Z^\top Z)_k,\; (Z^\top y)_k,\; (X^\top Z)_k,\; n_k\bigr)  =y⊤ya = y^\top y, b=X⊤yb = X^\top y, C=X⊤XC = X^\top X. Adding summaries across chunks element-wise addition; aggregate single summary computed pooled data.

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
code: library(cevcmm)

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: modest example: N=1200N = 1200 observations, K=1K = 1 varying covariate, q=4q = 4 independent random effects. ’ll split 3 nodes.
code: set.seed(23) N <- 1200L q <- 4L  t <- runif(N) x <- runif(N) Z <- matrix(rnorm(N * q), N, q)  beta_1_fun <- function(u) sin(2 * pi * u) alpha_true <- rnorm(q, sd = 0.5) y <- 2 + beta_1_fun(t) * x + as.vector(Z %*% alpha_true) +      rnorm(N, sd = 0.5)

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: “data KK machines” case. node: computes spline design matrix slice tt XX, calls node_summary() slice, ships resulting vcmm_ss object (small — KB regardless nkn_k) central node. central node sums summaries calls fit_from_summaries(). Build spline design . (real deployment, node construct piece design locally using n_basis, degree, knot placement — package handles automatically long inputs agree.) node produces summary: size summary independent nkn_k: Now fit summaries — pass list directly:
code: # Assign each observation to one of 3 nodes node_id <- sample.int(3L, N, replace = TRUE) splits  <- split(seq_len(N), node_id) lengths(splits) #>   1   2   3  #> 405 401 394 design <- build_vcmm_design(X = x, t = t) X_d    <- design$X_design # N rows x p columns; p = intercept + spline-basis coefficients, # with n_basis auto-picked from the sample size. dim(X_d) #> [1] 1200   15 summaries <- lapply(splits, function(idx) {   node_summary(y[idx], X_d[idx, , drop = FALSE], Z[idx, , drop = FALSE]) }) summaries[[1]] #> <vcmm_ss>  one-batch sufficient statistics #>   n_obs : 405 #>   p     : 15   (fixed-effects columns) #>   q     : 4   (random-effects columns) #>   a     : 2461 object.size(summaries[[1]]) #> 4776 bytes object.size(y[splits[[1]]]) + object.size(X_d[splits[[1]], ]) #> 52104 bytes ctrl <- vcmm_control(sigma_eps       = 0.5,                      sigma_alpha     = 0.5,                      update_variance = TRUE)  fit_dist <- fit_from_summaries(summaries,                                penalty = design$penalty,                                control = ctrl,                                method  = "ss",                                re_cov  = "diag") fit_dist #> <vcmm_fit>  Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Model fit #>   method      : SS #>   n_obs       : 1200 #>   p (fixed)   : 15 #>   q (random)  : 4 #>   RE cov      : diag #>   iterations  : 5 (converged) #>   sigma_eps   : 0.4845 #>   sigma_alpha : 0.7251 #>   elapsed     : 0.0020 sec

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: maximum coefficient difference sits BLAS summation-noise floor — typically near machine epsilon (∼10−15\sim 10^{-15}). distributed fit pooled fit; path produced differs.
code: fit_pooled <- vcmm(y, X = x, Z = Z, t = t,                    method = "ss", re_cov = "diag",                    control = ctrl)  # beta estimates max(abs(fit_pooled$beta - fit_dist$beta)) #> [1] 1.221245e-14  # alpha estimates max(abs(fit_pooled$alpha - fit_dist$alpha)) #> [1] 2.220446e-16  # variance components all.equal(fit_pooled$sigma_eps,   fit_dist$sigma_eps) #> [1] TRUE all.equal(fit_pooled$sigma_alpha, fit_dist$sigma_alpha) #> [1] TRUE

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: many small chunks don’t want keep memory, use accumulator instead list. accumulator stores running sum sufficient statistics; chunks can incorporated discarded arrive. Fit accumulator — call signature:
code: p <- ncol(X_d) acc <- init_accumulator(p = p, q = q)  # Pretend chunks arrive one at a time for (k in seq_along(splits)) {   idx <- splits[[k]]   ss_k <- compute_sufficient_stats(y[idx],                                    X_d[idx, , drop = FALSE],                                    Z[idx, , drop = FALSE])   acc <- accumulate_stats(acc, ss_k) } acc #> <vcmm_accumulator>  accumulated sufficient statistics #>   n_obs : 1200 #>   p     : 15   (fixed-effects columns) #>   q     : 4   (random-effects columns) #>   a     : 7587 fit_acc <- fit_from_summaries(acc, penalty = design$penalty,                               control = ctrl, method = "ss", re_cov = "diag")  # Same answer as Pattern 1 max(abs(fit_acc$beta - fit_dist$beta)) #> [1] 0

title: Distributed fitting with cevcmm
text: rowSums(Z) constant — example, row ZZ exactly one “origin” indicator one “destination” indicator, row sums s=2s = 2 — model one-dimensional identifiability redundancy. scalar cc, replacing α↦α+c𝟏,β0↦β0−cs \alpha \;\mapsto\; \alpha + c\mathbf{1}, \qquad \beta_0 \;\mapsto\; \beta_0 - c\,s  leaves Zα+β0𝟏Z\alpha + \beta_0\mathbf{1} — hence likelihood — unchanged. vcmm() detects automatically inspecting raw ZZ matrix applies centering shift ∑jα̂j=0\sum_j \hat\alpha_j = 0. fit_from_summaries() doesn’t auto-detect row-sum sufficient statistics, supply explicitly via rowsum_constant: Without rowsum_constant, distributed fit’s β̂0\hat\beta_0 α̂\hat\alpha differ pooled fit’s exactly centering shift. fits redundancy; choice representative within equivalence class differs. See vignette("od-migration", package = "cevcmm") full OD example.
code: # Skeleton — see vignette("od-migration") for a complete runnable example. # G and Sigma_spatial come from the OD setup (number of regions and an # initial spatial covariance matrix). fit_dist_od <- fit_from_summaries(   summaries, penalty = design$penalty, control = ctrl,   method = "ss", re_cov = "kronecker",   n_groups           = G,              # number of OD regions   Sigma_spatial_init = Sigma_spatial,  # G x G initial spatial covariance   rowsum_constant    = 2               # matches what vcmm() applies internally )

title: Getting started with cevcmm
text: cevcmm fits Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Models (VCMMs): yi=β0(ti)+∑k=1Kxikβk(ti)+zi⊤α+εi, y_i = \beta_0(t_i) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} x_{ik}\,\beta_k(t_i) + z_i^\top \alpha + \varepsilon_i, βk(t)\beta_k(t) smooth function tt (cubic B-splines second-order-difference penalty), α∼N(0,Σα)\alpha \sim N(0, \Sigma_\alpha) random effects one three covariance structures (diag, kronecker, separable), εi∼N(0,σε2)\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2). package implements two estimators Jalili Lin (2025): SS (sufficient-statistics): iterates convergence using aggregated summary (,b,C,Z⊤Z,Z⊤y,X⊤Z)(,\,b,\,C,\,Z^\top Z,\,Z^\top y,\,X^\top Z). Raw data needed summaries exist. CSL (one-step communication-efficient): single Newton refinement pilot estimator. Asymptotically equivalent SS fraction cost pilot good enough. vignette walks simplest case: single varying coefficient, diagonal random effects, one machine.

title: Getting started with cevcmm
text: N=500N = 500 observations, K=1K = 1 varying covariate, q=3q = 3 independent random effects.
code: set.seed(1) N <- 500L q <- 3L  t <- runif(N) x <- runif(N) Z <- matrix(rnorm(N * q), N, q)  beta_0     <- 2 beta_1_fun <- function(u) sin(2 * pi * u) alpha_true <- rnorm(q, sd = 0.4) sigma_eps  <- 0.5  y <- beta_0 + beta_1_fun(t) * x +      as.vector(Z %*% alpha_true) +      rnorm(N, sd = sigma_eps)

title: Getting started with cevcmm
text: varying_coef() evaluates β̂k(t)\hat\beta_k(t) grid optional pointwise standard errors. Estimated true varying coefficient. estimate tracks true sin⁡(2πt)\sin(2\pi t) across unit interval; band pointwise 95% Wald interval.
code: t_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100L) vc     <- varying_coef(fit, t_new = t_grid, k = 1L, se.fit = TRUE)  plot(t_grid, vc$fit, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",      xlab = "t", ylab = expression(hat(beta)[1](t)),      ylim = range(vc$fit - 2 * vc$se.fit,                   vc$fit + 2 * vc$se.fit,                   beta_1_fun(t_grid))) polygon(c(t_grid, rev(t_grid)),         c(vc$fit + 2 * vc$se.fit, rev(vc$fit - 2 * vc$se.fit)),         col = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.25), border = NA) lines(t_grid, beta_1_fun(t_grid), col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2) legend("topright", c("estimate", "truth"),        col = c("steelblue", "red"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: origin-destination (OD) model, observation flow two regions. Migration counts US states, commuting flows zip codes, trade flows countries fit pattern. random-effects design two indicators per observation: one origin region one destination region. GG regions, random-effects matrix ZZ N×2GN \times 2G, every row sums 2. natural covariance structure resulting 2G2G-vector random effects Kronecker form Σα=Σ2×2⏟origin/dest⊗Σspatial⏟regions, \Sigma_\alpha \;=\; \underbrace{\Sigma_{2\times 2}}_{\text{origin/dest}}                   \;\otimes\;                   \underbrace{\Sigma_{\text{spatial}}}_{\text{regions}},  Σ2×2\Sigma_{2\times 2} captures origin/destination variance plus covariance, Σspatial\Sigma_{\text{spatial}} (G×GG \times G) captures spatial dependence regions (often known priori geography). reduces 2G(2G+1)/22G(2G+1)/2 free covariance parameters just 3 Σ2×2\Sigma_{2\times 2} — substantial saving GG moderate.

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: single vcmm() call re_cov = "kronecker". package detects constant row-sum automatically applies identifiability shift described vignette("distributed-fitting").
code: fit <- vcmm(y = od$log_flow,             X = od$wage_diff,             Z = Z,             t = od$t,             method             = "csl",             re_cov             = "kronecker",             n_groups           = G,             Sigma_spatial_init = Sigma_spatial,             control            = vcmm_control(               sigma_eps       = 0.6,               sigma_alpha     = sqrt(0.5),               update_variance = TRUE)) fit #> <vcmm_fit>  Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Model fit #>   method      : CSL #>   n_obs       : 3000 #>   p (fixed)   : 19 #>   q (random)  : 20 #>   RE cov      : kronecker #>   pilot iter  : 4 (converged) #>   newton step : 1 #>   sigma_eps   : 0.5991 #>   Sigma_2x2   : #>     [0.9444  0.7649] #>     [0.7649  0.8597] #>   OD corr     : 0.8489 #>   Sigma_spatial: 10 x 10 (G = 10 groups) #>   elapsed     : 0.0060 sec (pilot 0.0030s + newton 0.0010s)

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: diagonal entries give origin destination variance components; -diagonal entry summarises whether region tends send many migrants also tends receive many. positive correlation says yes — high-traffic regions high-traffic directions. true simulation values Σ2×2=(0.600.250.250.50)\Sigma_{2\times2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.60 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 0.50 \end{pmatrix} (correlation 0.46). small GG, estimated Σ̂2×2\hat\Sigma_{2\times 2} differ truth. 3 free parameters Σ2×2\Sigma_{2\times 2} estimated effectively single G×2G \times 2 realisation MM, plus EM correction posterior uncertainty α\alpha given data. G=10G = 10, sampling variability empirical cor(M)\text{cor}(M) standard error ≈1/G−2≈0.35\approx 1/\sqrt{G-2} \approx 0.35, EM correction inflates diagonals account posterior uncertainty. qualitative pattern — positive variance components, positive OD correlation, “high-traffic” regions send receive heavily — right takeaway GG; exact numerical values rely larger network repeated realisations.
code: Sigma_2x2_hat <- fit$re_cov_state$Sigma_left round(Sigma_2x2_hat, 3) #>       [,1]  [,2] #> [1,] 0.944 0.765 #> [2,] 0.765 0.860  # Correlation between origin and destination effects corr_OD <- Sigma_2x2_hat[1, 2] /            sqrt(Sigma_2x2_hat[1, 1] * Sigma_2x2_hat[2, 2]) round(corr_OD, 3) #> [1] 0.849

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: internal column-stacking convention α=veccol(M)\alpha = \mathrm{vec}_{\text{col}}(M) MM G×2G \times 2 — first column holds origin effects, second holds destination effects. quick visual two effect series. Notice origin dest track closely across regions — ’s visual signature high empirical OD correlation discussed . Estimated origin destination effects per region.
code: alpha_hat <- fit$alpha M_hat <- matrix(alpha_hat, nrow = G, ncol = 2L) colnames(M_hat) <- c("origin", "dest") rownames(M_hat) <- paste0("region_", seq_len(G)) round(M_hat, 3) #>           origin   dest #> region_1   0.876  0.629 #> region_2   1.004  1.201 #> region_3   0.450 -0.041 #> region_4   0.109 -0.313 #> region_5   0.494  0.288 #> region_6   0.905  0.677 #> region_7  -0.887 -0.466 #> region_8  -1.426 -0.911 #> region_9  -0.903 -0.254 #> region_10 -0.778 -0.655 matplot(seq_len(G), M_hat, type = "b", pch = 19, lty = 1, lwd = 2,         col = c("steelblue", "darkorange"),         xlab = "Region", ylab = "Random effect") abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "grey60") legend("topright", c("origin", "dest"),        col = c("steelblue", "darkorange"), pch = 19, lwd = 2, bty = "n")

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: Estimated time-varying effect wage_diff log flow. wage-difference effect oscillates time: simulation follows 1.5sin⁡(2πt)1.5\sin(2\pi t). Unlike Σ2×2\Sigma_{2\times 2}, varying-coefficient curve supported N=3000N = 3000 observations tracks truth essentially perfectly across 30-year window.
code: t_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100L) vc     <- varying_coef(fit, t_new = t_grid, k = 1L, se.fit = TRUE)  plot(t_grid, vc$fit, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",      xlab = "t (year, normalised)",      ylab = expression(hat(beta)[1](t)),      ylim = range(vc$fit - 2 * vc$se.fit, vc$fit + 2 * vc$se.fit,                   1.5 * sin(2 * pi * t_grid))) polygon(c(t_grid, rev(t_grid)),         c(vc$fit + 2 * vc$se.fit, rev(vc$fit - 2 * vc$se.fit)),         col = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.25), border = NA) lines(t_grid, 1.5 * sin(2 * pi * t_grid),       col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2) legend("topright", c("estimate", "truth"),        col = c("steelblue", "red"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")

title: OD migration with Kronecker covariance
text: OD problem can fit distributed setting. node computes node_summary() slice ships result; central node aggregates calls fit_from_summaries() rowsum_constant = 2 identifiability shift matches vcmm() applies automatically: See vignette("distributed-fitting", package = "cevcmm") full explanation distributed API.
code: # Split N obs across 3 nodes node_id <- sample.int(3L, N, replace = TRUE) splits  <- split(seq_len(N), node_id)  design <- build_vcmm_design(X = od$wage_diff, t = od$t) X_d    <- design$X_design  summaries <- lapply(splits, function(idx)   node_summary(od$log_flow[idx],                X_d[idx, , drop = FALSE],                Z[idx, , drop = FALSE]))  fit_dist <- fit_from_summaries(   summaries,   penalty            = design$penalty,   control            = vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.6,                                     sigma_alpha = sqrt(0.5),                                     update_variance = TRUE),   method             = "csl",   re_cov             = "kronecker",   n_groups           = G,   Sigma_spatial_init = Sigma_spatial,   rowsum_constant    = 2 )  # Same answer as the pooled fit above, up to BLAS noise max(abs(fit$beta  - fit_dist$beta)) max(abs(fit$alpha - fit_dist$alpha))

title: cevcmm — Communication-Efficient Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Models

title: Build a B-spline second-order difference penalty matrix — build_penalty_matrix
text: Constructs symmetric \(p \times p\) penalty matrix \(\mathbf{P}_\lambda\) use penalised B-spline varying coefficients. single varying coefficient (n_blocks = 1, default), \(p = n\_basis + 1\); intercept (row/column 1) unpenalised, remaining block \(\lambda \mathbf{D}_2^\top \mathbf{D}_2\) \(\mathbf{D}_2\) second-order difference operator n_basis coefficients.

title: Build a B-spline second-order difference penalty matrix — build_penalty_matrix
text: n_basis Integer (\(\geq 3\)). Number B-spline basis columns per varying coefficient. lambda Non-negative numeric. Smoothing parameter \(\lambda\). n_blocks Integer (\(\geq 1\)). Number varying coefficients (covariates) sharing penalty structure. Default 1.

title: Build a B-spline second-order difference penalty matrix — build_penalty_matrix
text: symmetric (1 + n_blocks * n_basis) x (1 + n_blocks * n_basis) numeric matrix. Row/column 1 zero (intercept unpenalised).

title: Build a B-spline second-order difference penalty matrix — build_penalty_matrix
text: n_blocks > 1 (multiple covariates varying coefficient), result block-diagonal: one zero entry intercept, followed n_blocks copies n_basis x n_basis penalty block. total dimension 1 + n_blocks * n_basis. small ridge added block positive-definite, guarantee numerical stability downstream solves.

title: Build a B-spline second-order difference penalty matrix — build_penalty_matrix

what: Build the design matrix and penalty for a VCMM — build_vcmm_design
title: Build the design matrix and penalty for a VCMM — build_vcmm_design
text: Constructs fixed-effects design matrix matching penalty VCMM one varying coefficients. Given covariates X (n K) time/index vector t (length n), builds cubic--default B-spline basis B t assembles X_design = cbind(1, B * X[,1], B * X[,2], ..., B * X[,K]), dimension n (1 + K * n_basis); first column intercept, covariate gets n_basis-column block. penalty: (1 + K * n_basis) (1 + K * n_basis) block-diagonal second-order difference penalty matrix intercept unpenalised. natural multi-covariate generalisation single-covariate design used simulation code, matching paper's general VCMM specification.

title: Build the design matrix and penalty for a VCMM — build_vcmm_design
text: X Numeric n K matrix (length-n vector K = 1) covariates get varying coefficients t. t Numeric vector length n. variable coefficients vary smoothly (time, index, location, etc.). n_basis Integer (\(\geq\) degree + 1) NULL. Number B-spline basis columns per varying coefficient. NULL (default), chosen max(floor(n^(1/3)) + 4, 10) matching simulation code's rule thumb. degree Integer. B-spline degree (default 3 = cubic). lambda Non-negative numeric. Smoothing parameter penalty (default 1). normalize_t Logical. TRUE (default), t linearly mapped [0, 1] building basis. Set FALSE t already [0, 1].

B_spline: numeric n n_basis basis matrix t. internal_knots, boundary_knots: spline knot locations, recorded predictions new t can use basis. degree, n_basis, K, lambda: scalars recording design built. normalize_t, t_min, t_max: remap new t values prediction time.

what: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
text: Performs single Newton refinement pilot estimator using aggregated sufficient statistics, implementing one-step communication-efficient surrogate-likelihood (CSL) estimator Jalili Lin (2025, Section 3). normal linear VCMM update $$   \widehat{\theta}_{CSL}   = \widehat{\theta}_0   - K^{-1} \, g(\widehat{\theta}_0), $$ \(\theta = (\beta, \alpha)\), gradient \(g\) uses full aggregated stats, Hessian \(K\) also uses full aggregated stats prior augmentation. Theorem 3.1 paper shows whenever pilot estimator \(\sqrt{N}\)-consistent, one-step CSL estimator first-order equivalent full SS estimator.

title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
code: fit_csl(   stats,   penalty,   control = vcmm_control(),   pilot = NULL,   pilot_max_iter = 5L,   pilot_tol_beta = 0.001,   pilot_tol_alpha = 0.001,   re_cov_state = NULL )

title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
text: stats vcmm_ss vcmm_accumulator object containing aggregated sufficient statistics. penalty symmetric \(p \times p\) penalty matrix build_penalty_matrix(). control vcmm_control object fitting options. sigma_eps sigma_alpha entries provide initial variance values used internal pilot (pilot = NULL). pilot Optional vcmm_fit object use pilot estimator. NULL (default), internal SS pilot run. pilot_max_iter Integer. Maximum iterations internal SS pilot (default 5). Ignored pilot supplied. pilot_tol_beta Positive numeric. Loose tolerance internal SS pilot (default 1e-3). Ignored pilot supplied. pilot_tol_alpha Positive numeric. Loose tolerance internal SS pilot (default 1e-3). Ignored pilot supplied. re_cov_state Optional. internal random-effects covariance state object (NULL diagonal, constructed kronecker via vcmm() re_cov = "kronecker"). Passed internal SS pilot reused Newton-step Hessian. Advanced users typically reach re_cov_state via vcmm() rather calling fit_csl() directly.

title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
text: list class "vcmm_fit" fields fit_ss(), plus: pilot: vcmm_fit pilot used. pilot_elapsed_sec: pilot fitting time. step_elapsed_sec: Newton step time. method field "CSL".

title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
text: Pilot estimator. pilot = NULL (default), internal SS pilot run loose tolerances small number iterations (pilot_max_iter = 5, pilot_tol_beta = 1e-3, pilot_tol_alpha = 1e-3); defaults match dense simulation study paper. Setting pilot_max_iter = 1L gives cheapest possible pilot (OLS-like single-step pilot used origin-destination simulation), still \(\sqrt{N}\)-consistent normal linear case. Advanced users may pass vcmm_fit object via pilot argument – e.g. previously fitted fit_ss() result. Hessian. implementation uses full aggregated Hessian, reference-node curvature approximation \(\tilde K\) paper. two first-order equivalent conditions Theorem 3.1; full-aggregated form accurate natural default single-server fit, typical use case package.

title: Fit a VCMM via the one-step CSL estimator — fit_csl
code: set.seed(1) n <- 500; p <- 4; q <- 3 X <- cbind(1, matrix(rnorm(n * (p - 1)), n, p - 1)) Z <- matrix(rnorm(n * q), n, q) alpha_true <- rnorm(q, sd = 0.5) y <- as.vector(   X %*% c(2, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8) + Z %*% alpha_true + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) )  stats <- compute_sufficient_stats(y, X, Z) P     <- build_penalty_matrix(n_basis = p - 1, lambda = 0.1)  # Default: internal SS pilot (5 loose iterations) then one Newton step fit <- fit_csl(stats, P,                vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.5)) fit #> <vcmm_fit>  Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Model fit #>   method      : CSL #>   n_obs       : 500 #>   p (fixed)   : 4 #>   q (random)  : 3 #>   RE cov      : diag #>   pilot iter  : 3 (converged) #>   newton step : 1 #>   sigma_eps   : 0.5000 #>   sigma_alpha : 0.5000 #>   elapsed     : 0.0020 sec (pilot <0.001s + newton <0.001s)  # Cheapest pilot: single SS step fit_one <- fit_csl(stats, P,                    vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.5),                    pilot_max_iter = 1L)  # Advanced: user-supplied pilot my_pilot <- fit_ss(stats, P,                    vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.5,                                 max_iter = 3)) fit_user <- fit_csl(stats, P,                     vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.5),                     pilot = my_pilot)

code: set.seed(1) n <- 600 t  <- runif(n); x <- runif(n); Z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 4), n, 4) a_true <- rnorm(4, sd = 0.3) y <- 2 + sin(2 * pi * t) * x + as.vector(Z %*% a_true) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)  design <- build_vcmm_design(X = x, t = t) Xd     <- design$X_design ctrl   <- vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.3)  # Split into 3 nodes and aggregate idx_node <- sample.int(3, n, replace = TRUE) summaries <- lapply(seq_len(3), function(s) {   ii <- which(idx_node == s)   node_summary(y[ii], Xd[ii, , drop = FALSE], Z[ii, , drop = FALSE]) }) fit <- fit_from_summaries(summaries,                            penalty = design$penalty,                            control = ctrl)

title: Fit a VCMM via the sufficient-statistics estimator — fit_ss
text: iteration algorithm solves: beta-update: (C + P) beta = b - XtZ %*% alpha alpha-update: (ZtZ + (sigma_eps^2 / sigma_alpha^2) * ) alpha = Zty - t(XtZ) %*% beta linear systems solved via invert_matrix(), automatically dispatches solve() SVD pseudo-inverse depending dimension condition number. Convergence declared relative change beta alpha falls respective tolerances. control$update_variance TRUE, residual variance re-estimated iteration SS residual sum squares, random-effect variance re-estimated mean(alpha^2).

title: Fit a VCMM via the sufficient-statistics estimator — fit_ss
code: set.seed(1) n <- 200; p <- 4; q <- 3 X <- cbind(1, matrix(rnorm(n * (p - 1)), n, p - 1)) Z <- matrix(rnorm(n * q), n, q) alpha_true <- rnorm(q, sd = 0.5) y <- as.vector(   X %*% c(2, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8) + Z %*% alpha_true + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) )  # Build sufficient statistics and penalty stats <- compute_sufficient_stats(y, X, Z) P     <- build_penalty_matrix(n_basis = p - 1, lambda = 0.1)  # Fit with fixed variances fit <- fit_ss(stats, P,               vcmm_control(sigma_eps = 0.5, sigma_alpha = 0.5)) fit #> <vcmm_fit>  Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Model fit #>   method      : SS #>   n_obs       : 200 #>   p (fixed)   : 4 #>   q (random)  : 3 #>   RE cov      : diag #>   iterations  : 5 (converged) #>   sigma_eps   : 0.5000 #>   sigma_alpha : 0.5000 #>   elapsed     : <0.001 sec coef(fit) #>     beta_1     beta_2     beta_3     beta_4  #>  2.0268439  0.5047267 -0.2624521  0.8393673

what: Numerically stable matrix inversion with automatic method selection — invert_matrix
title: Numerically stable matrix inversion with automatic method selection — invert_matrix
text: Inverts square matrix \(\) using dispatch rule balances speed numerical stability: q < 100: try Cholesky (via invert_spd_cpp()), falling LU (invert_general_cpp()) finally SVD pseudo-inverse matrix positive-definite. q >= 100: route SVD pseudo-inverse path. default dense LAPACK split-merge variant (svd_pseudo_inverse(), paper Algorithm 2). user opts (see method argument ), iterative truncated SVD RSpectra::svds() used instead – faster matrix effective rank much smaller q, slower otherwise. Cholesky fast path roughly 2-3x faster original kappa() + solve() R path VCMM K matrices. See Day 17 Day 18 validation scripts inst/validation/ bit-equivalence timing details.

title: Numerically stable matrix inversion with automatic method selection — invert_matrix
text: Numeric square matrix invert. q Optional integer. Routing dimension used pick inversion strategy (defaults nrow()). Pass explicit value know curvature block meaningful dimension differs row count. verbose Logical. TRUE, print chosen method. use_cpp Logical. TRUE (default since Day 17), use RcppArmadillo Cholesky/LU backend. FALSE, use original pure-R path kappa() plus solve(). method Character string, one "auto" (default), "lapack", "rspectra". affects q >= 100 branch. "auto", routing checks getOption("cevcmm.use_rspectra", FALSE): TRUE RSpectra package installed, truncated-SVD path used; otherwise dense LAPACK split-merge SVD used. "lapack", always use dense LAPACK path (original Algorithm 2 behaviour). "rspectra", force truncated SVD via RSpectra; matrix turns full-rank, function silently falls back LAPACK results always correct.

title: Compute one node's sufficient-statistics summary — node_summary
text: transmitted. returned object contains six fixed-size arrays whose dimensions depend \(p = \mathrm{ncol}(X)\) \(q = \mathrm{ncol}(Z)\), never node-local sample size. typical VCMM \(p \approx 15\) \(q \approx 50\), one summary kilobytes regardless \(N_s\). Basis alignment. nodes must build local X (spline-expanded fixed-effects design) using basis specification. recommended workflow call build_vcmm_design full t-range (pre-agreed knots), broadcast resulting basis, node evaluate local t. package enforce ; mis-aligned bases silently give incorrect fits.

title: Fit a Varying Coefficient Mixed-Effects Model — vcmm
text: Model. observations \(= 1, \ldots, n\) fitted model $$   y_i   = \beta_0(t_i) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} x_{ik}\, \beta_k(t_i)   + z_i^\top \alpha + \varepsilon_i, $$ \(\beta_k(t)\) cubic B-spline n_basis basis functions second-order difference penalty, \(\alpha \sim N(0, \Sigma_\alpha)\) structure chosen re_cov. Method selection. default method = "auto", picks "csl" \(N \cdot q > 10^5\) \(q > 50\) "ss" otherwise. Random-effects covariance. Three structures: re_cov = "diag": \(\alpha \sim N(0,     \sigma_\alpha^2 I_q)\). re_cov = "kronecker": \(\alpha \sim N(0,     \Sigma_{\mathrm{left}} \otimes \Sigma_{\mathrm{right}})\) \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{left}}\) size q_left x q_left (default q_left = 2; OD-style origin / destination blocks). User-facing names Sigma_2x2_init, Sigma_spatial_init accepted aliases. re_cov = "separable": \(\alpha \sim N(0,     \Sigma_q \otimes \Omega_G)\) Sigma_q size q_left x q_left (required, default) Omega_G size \(G \times G\). User-facing names Sigma_q_init, Omega_G_init accepted aliases Sigma_left_init, Sigma_right_init. Column-stacking convention. re_cov = "kronecker" "separable", random-effects vector \(\alpha = \mathrm{vec}_{\mathrm{col}}(M)\) \(M \\mathbb R^{G \times q_{\mathrm{left}}}\), .e.\ alpha[(k - 1) * G + g] = M[g, k]. Z matrix must constructed Z %*% alpha gives correct random-effect contribution. ncol(Z) must equal q_left * n_groups. Identifiability right component. right-side covariance (Sigma_spatial / Omega_G) separately identifiable single \(\hat\alpha\), held fixed user-supplied initial value (default \(I_G\)). Supply parametric kernel via Sigma_right_init (e.g., exp(-D / phi) OD; AR(1) separable). left-side covariance (Sigma_2x2 / Sigma_q) updated every iteration via EM-style estimator (Theorem 1 \(M_\eta\) rule) control$update_variance = TRUE. Choosing re_cov. three modes specify assumed covariance structure random-effects vector \(\alpha\); constrain Z's entries look like. distribution Z (binary indicators, continuous values, mixed) enter choice — structure \(\alpha\) . Pick data shape: "diag": independent random effects, one per group subject, assumed cross-group dependence. Simplest case. Use group contributes single offset groups exchangeable. "kronecker": origin-destination flow data. Every row Z activates one origin indicator one destination indicator, ncol(Z) = 2 * G \(G\) number regions. 2x2 left block captures origin/destination dependence; \(G \times G\) right block captures spatial dependence regions. "separable": group carries multiple correlated random effects (q_left > 2) — example random intercept plus random slope per region. Use per-group random-effect dimension exceeds 2.

Designed origin-destination flow data. re_cov = "separable": internal Kronecker machinery kronecker mode, q_left argument required (default) right-side ΩG\Omega_G held fixed initial value.
